narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive dedication in pursuing this path despite the challenges many face, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from past students—like their early and later school grades, undergraduate GPA, LSAT scores, family income levels, and whether they plan full-time studies—to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you, it estimated a 61% probability of failing the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for denial, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to succeed right away. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3rd, which is below the typical 5th or 6th decile for others and signaled weaker performance later in your studies compared to peers. Close behind, your LSAT score of 33—lower than the average 37—also nudged the prediction toward failure, as higher scores strongly correlate with bar success in the data. Your middle family income bracket played a moderate role in the same direction, differing from the broader spread seen in successful applicants. On a brighter note, your outstanding undergraduate GPA of 3.90—well above the 3.21 average—strongly pulled the prediction toward passing, showing your college excellence as a key strength. Your Year 1 decile of 4th had a smaller push toward failure, while planning full-time studies offered a slight positive boost, aligning with the vast majority of those who pass. Overall, your profile stands out with that exceptional GPA amid some areas below averages, but the model weighed the lower Year 3 performance and LSAT most heavily against students who typically pass. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar hurdles through targeted prep. Next, I recommend focusing on boosting your LSAT with practice tests and courses, strengthening later academic showings if possible, and exploring programs with bar passage support tailored for diverse students like you. You have the GPA foundation to build on; consider reapplying after addressing these with concrete improvements, and reach out to mentors who can guide Black women navigating law school admissions. Keep pushing forward—your story is far from over.
